keyLearnings: ~75% of 84 external sources are namedrop-only. Pattern extraction must be systematic --- quality gate catches NEW additions, but existing namedrops require manual extraction.
The macro-to-micro funnel (System -> Domain -> Module -> Root Cause) works for source integration analysis. Each source maps to a specific subsystem, file, and pattern.
WARN (not ERROR) is the right severity for citation checks. Blocking commits creates workarounds; visibility without friction is the goal.
Pattern extraction beats citation. A 5-line pattern from Aider carries more value than 66 namedrop citations combined.
Three parallel memory stores is correct architecture when availability guarantees differ. The fix is boundary definitions + unified query + one-way sync, not unification.
L3 content extraction from large skills follows the 80/20 rule: ~56% of inline lines can move to reference files.
Worktree isolation is foundational for autonomous git. Every successful agentic git system uses worktree-per-session with auto-commit on exit.
The tool vs loop boundary is the right split between main (tools) and fork (loops). git-agent.sh is a tool; autopilot.sh is a loop.
residualRisk: git-agent.sh tested locally but not with live gh auth for PR creation. goal-decompose.sh timed out waiting for agent (needs longer poll time). safety-guard.sh and goal-decompose.sh are committed to main but never invoked in production. The fork branches (feat/autonomous-runtime, feat/git-agent) have not been smoke-tested end-to-end. 46 remaining namedrop sources are acknowledged as completeness ceiling --- no value in extraction.
immediateNextSteps: Set up worktree for feat/autonomous-runtime branch to work on autonomous loop in parallel
Consider creating a PR to merge feat/git-agent back to main when stable
